en.title: TypeScript Basic Types
en.description: Basic Types in TypeScript allow you to define the types of variables, making code safer and more readable. They include string, number, boolean, any, array, tuple, void, null, and undefined.

content.text: Basic Types in TypeScript are the fundamental types used to specify what values a variable can hold. They are defined using the `:` symbol. For example, `let name: string = "Murad";` ensures the variable only holds strings. Basic types help TypeScript catch errors when incorrect values are used, making code more reliable.

Basic Types are assigned to variables and functions to ensure correct behavior. The most common basic types are: `string` (text), `number` (numbers), `boolean` (true/false), `any` (any type), `array` (lists), `tuple` (fixed-length lists), `void` (functions returning nothing), `null`, and `undefined` (empty or undefined values). TypeScript warns you if you use an incorrect type.

Basic types are very useful in real projects. For example, in an online store, you can use them to define a product‚Äôs name (string), price (number), or availability (boolean). You could also use a tuple to store a user‚Äôs name and age together. Below is a simple store example using basic types.

Basic types are a core feature of TypeScript, addressing JavaScript‚Äôs lack of type safety. In JavaScript, variable types are unknown until runtime, which can cause errors. Basic types in TypeScript allow you to define types upfront, catching errors during development. This is used in large projects by companies like Microsoft, Google, and Airbnb to improve code quality.

items.description: Integer or decimal numbers, e.g., `23` or `3.14`.
Accepts any type, but should be used sparingly.
Lists of values of the same type, e.g., `number[]`.
Fixed-length arrays with different types, e.g., `[string, number]`.
For functions that return no value.

content.text:
- **Dangers**: Overusing `any` reduces TypeScript‚Äôs benefits, as it bypasses type checking. Using too many different types can make code complex.
- **Recommendations**: Avoid `any`, use simple and precise types, define arrays and tuples correctly, and use an IDE like VSCode to catch errors early.
